serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gong Ting Zhua Chao Yu Pian is approximately 250 g, comprising batter-coated fish slices glazed in sweet-sour sauce, served as a shared main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, battered and fried, with sweet-sour sauce — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried fish (NDB 15264) and sweet-sour sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy) with proportional component adjustment
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Gong Ting Zhua Chao Yu Pian exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for battered fried fish (approximately 65% of dish weight) with sweet-sour sauce components (vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, starch; approximately 35% of dish weight). Protein and fat values are anchored to USDA SR Legacy 'Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat' (NDB 15264) adjusted for batter and sauce contribution. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of battered fried fish with starchy sweet-sour sauce — analogous estimate based on 'Fish fillet, battered and fried' and 'Sweet and sour sauce' GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gong Ting Zhua Chao Yu Pian. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: battered fried fish contributes a low-to-medium GI base (fish protein and fat lower GI), while the sweet-sour sauce with added sugar and starch raises the overall GI. Composite estimate of GI 55 is consistent with similar Chinese sweet-sour fish preparations. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 13.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 19.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1050 mg per serving) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a full 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 55) and significant added sugar in sweet-sour sauce (~6.5 g sugar/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of the added sugar in the sweet-sour sauce; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 16 g of sugar and a glycemic load of 19, which is moderate — pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion to 150 g can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat from frying (7.2 g fat/100 g) with relatively low saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g); beneficial omega-3 from fish partially offset by frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish base provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but deep-frying and stir-frying in oil increase total fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer a smaller portion (100–150 g) and note that the sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) also poses a secondary cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gong Ting Zhua Chao Yu Pian (宫廷抓炒鱼片, Imperial Stir-Fried Sliced Fish) is a classic dish from Chinese imperial court cuisine, historically associated with the Qing Dynasty and reportedly a favourite of Empress Dowager Cixi. The dish features boneless white fish fillets that are coated in a light egg-and-starch batter, deep-fried until golden and crisp, then quickly stir-fried in a glossy sweet-sour sauce made from vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, ginger, and scallion. The result is a dish with a tender, flaky fish interior, a lightly crisp exterior, and a vibrant tangy-sweet glaze. The flavour profile is characterised by a balanced interplay of sour and sweet notes with a savoury umami undertone from soy sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 168 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat, and 13.8 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notable at approximately 420 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), driven by the starchy batter and added sugar in the sauce. The dish is a good source of lean protein and provides vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus from the fish. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and may contain gluten if wheat starch is used in the batter. In Singapore, this dish is served in Chinese restaurants specialising in Beijing or imperial-style cuisine.
